title.title: Introduction to Smart Contracts
title.subtitle: Fundamentals, history, and the essential blockchain knowledge you need

warmup.heading: Bitcoin or Ethereum?
warmup.subtitle: Recall before we build. Tag each statement, then see the answer.

items.why: Bitcoin was the first blockchain. Ethereum came in 2015.

items.stmt: Launched in 2015 by Vitalik Buterin & co.
items.why: Ethereum mainnet went live July 2015 with the Solidity language.
Bitcoin has a fixed 21M supply. ETH has no hard cap (~120M circulating).
Ethereum runs arbitrary logic on the EVM. Bitcoin Script is intentionally limited.

items.stmt: Tracks funds with the UTXO model
items.why: Bitcoin spends discrete unspent outputs. Ethereum uses account balances.
Ethereum mutates a balance per account. Bitcoin uses UTXOs.

items.stmt: Switched to Proof of Stake in 2022
items.why: The Merge moved Ethereum to PoS. Bitcoin still uses Proof of Work.

items.stmt: A public, decentralised ledger where the native coin pays fees
items.why: True of both — BTC pays Bitcoin fees, ETH pays Ethereum gas.

recap.heading: Quick Recap — Ethereum, the World Computer
recap.leadA: You met all of this in
recap.leadB: . A 60-second refresher before we go deeper into the contracts themselves.

cards.title: Accounts
cards.desc: Two kinds: EOAs controlled by a private key (people), and contract accounts controlled by their code.

cards.title: The EVM
cards.desc: Every node runs the same Ethereum Virtual Machine, so a program produces the same result everywhere.

cards.title: Gas
cards.desc: You pay for computation. Gas prices each step and guarantees programs always halt — no infinite loops.

cards.title: Transactions
cards.desc: Signed messages that change global state. Only an EOA can start one; it can call a contract.

recap.footerB: a smart contract. The next sections are all about what that code can do, how to read it, and when it's the right tool.

what.heading: What is a Smart Contract?
what.subtitle: A program that lives on a blockchain — and runs exactly as written, every time.
what.defLeadB: is a self-executing program stored on a blockchain whose terms are written directly in code — not in natural language.
what.defBody: When predefined conditions are met, the contract executes automatically. No intermediary. No manual trigger. No possibility of censorship or tampering.

props.title: Autonomous
props.desc: Executes automatically when conditions are satisfied. No human needs to trigger or approve anything after deployment.

props.title: Immutable
props.desc: Once deployed to the blockchain, the code cannot be changed. What you deploy is what runs — forever.

props.title: Transparent
props.desc: Anyone can read the contract code on-chain. There are no hidden clauses — the logic is fully public and auditable.

props.title: Deterministic
props.desc: Given the same inputs, a smart contract always produces the same output. Every node on the network agrees on the result.

props.title: Trustless
props.desc: Parties do not need to trust each other — they only need to trust the code. The protocol enforces every term.

szabo.heading: Nick Szabo's Vending Machine
szabo.subtitle: The original analogy — and the mind behind the concept, 30 years before DeFi.
szabo.quote: "A smart contract is a set of promises, specified in digital form, including protocols within which the parties perform on these promises."

whyPoints.a: He understood that contracts are fundamentally

whyPoints.b: (1998) — a direct precursor to Bitcoin's design

szabo.imgAlt: Smart contract metaphor: a vending machine. Insert conditions → automated result. No middleman.
szabo.insightA: the machine doesn't care who you are. No trust, no negotiation, no middleman. The code

history.heading: Historical Evolution of Smart Contracts
history.subtitle: From an idea with no computer to run it, to $100B+ in live contracts.

events.title: Szabo coins "smart contract"
events.desc: Promises enforced by code — the vending-machine analogy, 15 years before any chain could run it.

events.title: Bitcoin — first on-chain contracts
events.desc: Script does conditional spending (multisig, timelocks) — deliberately non-Turing-complete.

events.title: Ethereum + Solidity
events.desc: The Turing-complete EVM ships: deploy arbitrary contract logic to a shared, trustless computer.

events.title: The DAO — first contract crisis
events.desc: A reentrancy bug drains $60M; Ethereum forks (ETH/ETC). Security becomes a discipline.

events.title: ERC-20 — token contracts
events.desc: A shared token interface every wallet & exchange understands. The ICO boom follows.

events.title: ERC-721 — NFT contracts
events.desc: A standard for unique, owned tokens — assets plus automatic on-transfer royalties.

events.title: DeFi Summer
events.desc: Contracts calling contracts — "money legos." Lending, trading & yield, no intermediary.

events.title: Audited, multi-chain, high-stakes
events.desc: $100B+ in contracts across chains; audits mandatory — $6B+ lost to exploits since 2016.

exercise.heading: Is this a Smart Contract?
exercise.subtitle: Click YES or NO for each scenario, then see the explanation.

scenarios.title: Vending Machine
scenarios.reason: Self-executing logic with automatic conditional payout — the original analogy Szabo used.

scenarios.title: Airbnb Escrow
scenarios.desc: Platform holds payment until host confirms check-in
scenarios.reason: Conditional fund release based on a confirmed event — a textbook smart contract use case.

scenarios.title: Hospital Records
scenarios.desc: Store & share medical history across doctors
scenarios.reason: Data is private, frequently updated, and legally sensitive. On-chain storage is 50,000× more expensive than a database.

scenarios.title: Concert Ticket Resale
scenarios.desc: NFT tickets — artist royalty enforced on every resale
scenarios.reason: Automatic royalty on every transfer is impossible in traditional ticketing — smart contracts enforce it natively.

scenarios.title: Company Expenses
scenarios.reason: Internal process, single trusted party. No decentralisation needed — a simple approval workflow is cheaper and faster.

scenarios.title: Crop Insurance
scenarios.desc: Auto-payout when rainfall falls below threshold
scenarios.reason: Parametric insurance: oracle provides data, contract executes payout. No claims adjuster, no delay.

scenarios.title: Social Media Likes
scenarios.desc: Track likes and followers in real-time
scenarios.reason: Millisecond updates + massive data volume. Gas costs alone would make each like cost dollars.

scenarios.title: DAO Treasury
scenarios.desc: Token holders vote; funds released if proposal passes
scenarios.reason: Multi-party conditional payment with no board or legal entity needed. This is exactly what DAOs do.

scenarios.title: Streaming Service
scenarios.desc: Pay monthly fee to access video library
scenarios.reason: Storing a 2-hour movie on-chain would cost billions. Off-chain storage + access token is the right model.

scenarios.title: Trade Finance
scenarios.desc: Letter of credit: pay importer when goods are confirmed delivered
scenarios.reason: Multiple untrusting parties + conditional cross-border payment. Banks already pilot this with Hyperledger.

quiz.question: Nick Szabo introduced the concept of smart contracts in 1994. Which real-world object did he use as the foundational analogy to explain how they work?
options: A notary office — a trusted third party that validates and records agreements between parties.
A vending machine — a mechanism that autonomously enforces the terms of a transaction without a human intermediary.
A court of law — an authority that resolves disputes and enforces contractual obligations after the fact.
A bank vault — a secure system that holds assets until conditions agreed by both parties are met.
quiz.explanation: Szabo used the vending machine as his canonical analogy: you insert money, select an item, and the machine automatically verifies the payment and delivers the product — no cashier, no trust, no dispute. The machine's logic is the contract. Szabo's insight was that this same principle — conditional logic enforced by a mechanism rather than a person — could be embedded in digital protocols. This became the intellectual foundation for Ethereum's smart contract model two decades later.

takeaways.title: Section 01 — Key Takeaways
items: A smart contract is self-executing code on a blockchain — no intermediary can stop or alter it
Nick Szabo described the concept in 1994; Bitcoin (2009) and Ethereum (2015) made it real
Key properties: autonomous, immutable, transparent, deterministic, trustless
The vending machine model: conditions → automatic execution, no human judgment needed
DeFi, NFTs, and DAOs are all smart contracts at different scales
$6B+ lost to exploits — the power of smart contracts comes with serious security responsibility

summary.heading: Section Summary
summary.subtitle: Everything covered in this section — at a glance

cards.title: Nick Szabo (1994)
cards.summary: "Contractual clauses embedded in hardware and software" — the original idea, 20 years before Ethereum

cards.title: Bitcoin Scripts
cards.summary: Limited but valid smart contracts: multi-sig, time locks, HTLCs — intentionally not Turing-complete

cards.title: Ethereum (2015)
cards.summary: Turing-complete EVM enabled arbitrary smart contracts, opening DeFi, NFTs, and DAOs

cards.title: Key Properties
cards.summary: Immutable, autonomous, transparent, trust-minimized, deterministic — all enforced by the protocol

cards.title: Code is Law Limits
cards.summary: Bugs (The DAO: $60M), oracle failure, no legal standing — code is law until it isn't

cards.title: When to Use
cards.summary: Multiple untrusting parties + conditional logic + no trusted intermediary = smart contract territory

summary.footer: Proceed to Section 2 to explore the Smart Contract ecosystem →
